speaker: interviewer
text: March the 4th, 1996. Jerry Goose, tape number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jerry, how long were you with the Jewish families in the Okna woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about a year and a half, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what started, what happened. [INAUDIBLE]. We was liberated already two weeks, and we don't know that. We was in the woods. The only thing we heard, Jews, they were shot, bombs and that. And last week it was so quiet, and we wondered why it's so quiet, why is not no shooting going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once in [INAUDIBLE] was families. They went to the Polish people. There was a village of Polish people. And they asked, what's the matter, so quiet? He said, the Russians already. The Germans not anymore here. So then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, then we went to the city. Was [INAUDIBLE]. From Fanny's town, it's 12 mile to Grzymalow. It's another city. We came over to them. And then everybody went from where they come. Some came from Skalat. There was all kind of people. And some was killed then. Between them and where me was, they was killed a lot of people too. Because the Ukrainians, they found out where me are. In the night they start shooting, and they killed a lot of people with us. They left maybe about 20 people in the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the group what was. And then we came to the city. In the city was no Jews, nobody was in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grzymalow. Grzymalow, they call in Polish. In fact, we was afraid to be there. We said, maybe we should go back in the woods. Then what me did, we was in the City Hall. We walked in the City Hall. Nobody there. Was nobody. And one decide one should stay watch in the night. And then I say in the second day, there's already come Russians coming into the city. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's all what I could tell you about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened with the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what happen, we come in Russian, they have a special office to get people from the city, and from the villages, to the army. And they took me to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you volunteer, or did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They took me like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me. Then when they toss us in [? buggies ?] and drive us to the [? center ?] in the army, then the Ukrainians started shooting at us. And then I got-- there. And they take my father away, the Russians, by Kiev in a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after your hand recovered?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about maybe a year, eight months--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hospital. But the finger, when it's now, I would have the finger. I tell them, the finger, one like that and one on left. Of course, I was here, too, but this was not as bad. Used to tell them, doctor. She was a Jewish doctor, too. I say, doctor, why not leave my other fingers?

speaker: interviewer
text: They say, you know what? I would like to. First, we haven't got enough things to heal your fingers. And then, you see, the Germans' bullets, when they hit something, they get in little, little pieces. You couldn't even take out and leave the finger and then sew it together. Maybe the bullet's going to be still in your arm. Then we will suggest you let us take it off. And I say, what I could do? Nobody could suggest me nothing, I couldn't say nothing. It was a table like this one, like mine, in living room. They said me lay down on the table, and they put in something under my neck. And they say, you count to 20. They gave me some narcotic. And I was counting to 20, and I fall asleep. And after I wake up, they say, you going to walk to your bed? I say, yes. I walk to the bed. And then they give me-- I remember like today-- they gave me a nice piece cake. See, after this sickness I could eat the cake. I say, yeah, OK, give me the cake. And I put away in a side table.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wake up already, the cake is gone. I said, give me the cake. He said if you're happy, I'll give you a bread. They are sick people, they stole the cakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then? After you got out of the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was, like I say, a [YIDDISH]. You know what the-- I'm [NON-ENGLISH]. No home to go, no work to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the war over by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the war over, all over?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war is not all over, no. There was still fighting some. I could go to Berezne, of course, my city. Then-- yes. Between time, I was [LAUGHS] You know when you are a businessman, even in hospital, you're doing business too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I used to buy some paper rolls, papers, in this, in-- what they call them? Tobacco. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I used to go out in the village and buy them and give to the sick one, and the sick-- not buy, they give me sugar. To go out to the village and trade for the-- Tobacco?

speaker: interviewee
text: --tobacco. Between time, I make profit, maybe $20, $30. And I planned to, when I go home. I'm not home. I go to buy some, the Russian people like the headscarves. Buying a few headscarves and that, and maybe I sell it and make money. Well, I was thinking this was not a little thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on the train, and I got my passport together, and the conductor have a trick. He said, we're gonna stay by the station maybe about a half an hour. You could go down. We went down, about maybe 20 people. And what they did? They gone. With everything. Everybody was crying. They all from the army people, you know? They got, everybody got some. They giving you pants, they give you everything. I was left naked. In between time, I meet a guy, he came from, what do they call them? Russian he was. They took him away in the time from the war on, what you call where that is? I forgot. Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Siberia. And they was at the station. And they looked at me and see I've been so depressed. They say, listen, why are you depressed? You see, everything mine has gone with the train. And there I got tricked like that. And it was maybe four people more, they took everything away, and they're gone. Then they gave me $50-- 50 rubles. I got enough to buy food. I got tickets, they gave me in the hospital. But to buy some other things. And then I made the first thing I went to my Berezne and looked. And I came, it's one big pile with people killed. And that's-- I never went anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you were when the official German ceasefire was, when Germany surrendered? Do you remember that day?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, when Germany surrendered. Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Skalat, where my wife is.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Skalat? Mm-hmm. Is that where you went after you left Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in fact, I-- no. I was still business. I got $50 then after [INAUDIBLE]. Well, not 50, maybe 30. I went to Kiev, then Berezne. I got free-- go, I don't need tickets. And I find out they need the yeast to make vodka. And I bought for $20 a package yeast and brought it there to [INAUDIBLE] in [? Skalat ?] where they make the vodka. And give me then $100, $75. And I went again, and this way I start making business. I start making enough money to-- and then I meet Fanny in this city what I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you happen to come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's another little story, yeah? We was-- we had decided to-- maybe Fanny tell you already-- emigrate from the Russians' places where the Russians, and go farther to where Poland is. And then we went from city to city. So let me think. We got the radios. Yes, they put-- we got in a camp where there used to be-- before the Germans left the camp, which Jewish people used to be in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not-- it used to be, maybe, a labor camp. But then there were Jewish people would run away, go back in Poland, and they give them a place where they have to emigrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: A DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a DP camp. It was then maybe about 200 people. That was a [INAUDIBLE] and some other thing. And that's what I survived there for the time be.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and Fanny together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Fanny never tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, Fanny didn't tell me, it's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pocking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pocking camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? INT: Pocking ?] camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were all [? from-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: They were [INAUDIBLE] Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: There were [? from ?] people, just [? from ?] people in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, was Lubavitch. They really did theirself troubled. The Lubavitch is supposed to go to Israel. And they bought refrigeration and other things. And what happened? Saturday fall down, they prayed in a room. But then a candle fall out. It was hot. And nobody was afraid to put out the light. Saturday I put the light. And there was barracks maybe with 100 people. Everything went on fire. Everything burned. Just the people alive were run away alive in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you happen to come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, but that's-- later I find out-- Fanny knows that she has a sister in the United States. And she was-- and she write to the sister. And she [? command ?] the sister, I don't believe [INAUDIBLE] she's her sister or not. Because she was writing to her, and she answered her back. What letter, she said, who she are? And who are her father and mother? And then she accepted her sister. Then [INAUDIBLE] she write a letter, me want to go over to United States or to Canada. I wouldn't stay in Germany camps. She said, OK. I think what Jerry has some relative in Detroit. I go speak to the relative, and me see what me could do. Then, I find out another thing, I got some relative in Winnipeg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know how me find out the Winnipeg, the address. Fanny, I don't know how she found out. Anyway, to make the story short, I find out I got two uncles and cousins there in Winnipeg. And she found out who they are, and she's writing to them a letter. I'm Jerry Goose, I came from Berezne, and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we get a letter from my uncle. Let him tell in writing, who is his father? Who was in Berezne? What he did, his father? What this, and everything he should tell them. And we tell them everything. Then they write a letter, is more diplomatic. And we're going to try to bring him over. And they started working together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as it happens, it was not this easy. Because we couldn't take off just like that. We have to be our relative. And she used to have a aunt in Toronto. There, she-- they have to make something up. She's more or less the age, when the [INAUDIBLE] put us two years older they made her. And this way we-- they put in two places application. In the United States of America and to the Canada. And I was twice, and they didn't want to accept me. Because they say we got enough welfare people. We don't need your husband on welfare now. And they called the end. Then they don't want to accept me, then I was writing again to my uncle to Winnipeg. He say, don't worry. You've got two uncles and cousins, and we're going to try to bring you over.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London. I could tell you, I mean, go and ask Fanny. But I couldn't remember the correct date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you-- is London the only place you've ever lived since you've been in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We came Quebec. From Quebec, we decided to go to Toronto because-- to Winnipeg, somebody give us advice, too far for no lover. Mine say, you go to Toronto, in Toronto you've got-- she said I got an aunt in Toronto. That's good. Then we went to Toronto, and she called up the aunt. And a cousin picked us up and brought us up to the aunt. And there I started looking for a job. And I couldn't find a job there. Then my auntie find out they're looking for a butcher in London. She-- and a butcher used to be [INAUDIBLE], which I don't-- you know [INAUDIBLE]. And I used-- and they said they need a kosher Yiddish butcher. Then I came there. Then when I don't know English, I go, and now I go on the train. They put me on a little paper. They said, you come to the station, show the conductor the paper. Come to the station, show the conductor the paper. Then I come too-- he said, when you come-- when you come at the station London, show a taxi man the paper. I show a taxi man the paper. And the aunt is Fanny's cousin [? Salabi. ?] Maybe you heard of her. She used to live on Williams Street. And they give me the paper to come, to here, to sleep over, he accept me. I was not sure he's going to accept me. Then she was not there, and it was just her son Jerry. Maybe you know him, don't know. He's a lawyer in Toronto. Then, he don't know Jewish [YIDDISH], and I don't Jewish English. He speak to me in English, I know nothing. He said, spell it. When I don't know English, I couldn't spell it to you. Well, then I sit till 11:00, 12:00. She come for the bag. And she spoke Yiddish. And then her husband drive me over to Lubavitch and accept me. Between time, Fanny's still in Toronto with her aunt. And later when they accept me, I brought over Fanny to London. And it was Rabbi Kirschenbaum, he found us a job by Jack. Mrs. Jack on Simcoe Street got a little room, one room, a little room upstairs, and you could rent it for $10 a week. Then we were renting this little place until we decided farther where to go. And he gave me $35 a week that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think your experiences in the war changed you? What effect do they think they had on you, Jerry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say-- I don't think I got affected nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I wouldn't say nothing. Is there-- is there a message or anything that you learned or that you'd like to give?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't give nothing because everything happened soonly. The Russians [INAUDIBLE], the Germans [INAUDIBLE], and I start going in the woods and hiding and this. What message I could give people? I was not a good time, I was not in business. I couldn't suggest you what to do. There's no messages I could give you, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to give your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I decide?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, what made you decide to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the same-- I decided because I want my grandchildren-- of course, they know a little bit. They know what I was-- I was through the war. Of course, they don't want to listen to it these days. My kids, Martin and Steven, they don't ask me question to how I was, what I was through, nothing. He could listen what I say, stories for somebody. But not special asking me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like your grandchildren to know? What lesson would you like them to learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing I could suggest them, look for the government. When the government anti-Semitic, run from them. What everything do, the government do. Nobody do it. Hitler did. And everything what they did. When see something is wrong, friends-- I'm sorry to say, me haven't got too many friends. In Canada, I don't feel anti-Semitic in Canada. Just, I could feel out. It was not all my friends. In fact, I tell the truth. Were not the Canadian people, I wouldn't have the house, I wouldn't have cars, I wouldn't have everything. But some is not friend. They all friends just like the Ukrainians was. When was no war, [INAUDIBLE] was a lot of Ukrainian friends. We used to work together, me used to go the night, sleep with them. But then later, in the war, they killed us. I got Ukrainian boys friends, and then when they come, the Germans, they don't know me. That's what I say, soon as the government change, run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not what I think more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Jerry.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Thank you for the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that a picture of, Jerry?

speaker: interviewee
text: What one?

speaker: interviewer
text: The one that we're looking at on the monitor.

speaker: interviewee
text: There?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my [YIDDISH], my mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to get a picture of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same way when I got a picture of my dad. I was in Winnipeg. And I walked into my cousin in Winnipeg. And on the walls, everyone in either in the factory hang the picture. And I say, maybe you got something that I could get the picture. A picture. So that I could [INAUDIBLE] a little. And then I made it in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your [YIDDISH] Isaac do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not do nothing, just he had to have a hardware store. He was a [INAUDIBLE] shop. He was closed Saturday and work [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know how-- anything about when that picture was taken or how it happened to be taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm sorry I couldn't tell you. The reason, I just remembered. Me used to sell writing letters for help. I don't know when they send it that little picture for that. See, now he never was to gray hair, too. And I was gray hair early. I don't know that is a picture. Just me looked in the album in Detroit, and I saw the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And tell me about that picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what to tell you about that picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the picture when we brought in the Torah in the synagogue Beth Tefilah what I donated. And there I rolled out the Torah, and everybody put in their name in the Torah, and they donate some money. In fact, there was donated about $2,000. For the guests, we came and made a party. And everybody signed their name. And I rolled out the Torah to see it. You could write.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the man from Histadrut, I forgot his name, too, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Histadrut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Histadrut.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was work for Histadrut. Mr. Kirschenbaum was the main chairman, the short one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job with the Histadrut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me go and collect money for Histadrut. Just arranged money make, to save money up. You know, with the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to say about any of these people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know what to say. The only thing we was good friends, and we was meetings coming up every month, two months. And we got meetings and decided what we should do and how we should do it [INAUDIBLE]. In fact, I got some-- I got the other thing, I'll show you later. The special from Israel, they sent me out [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much, Jerry.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
